coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of fluorescent silicon quantum dots for ultra-rapid and selective sensing of Cr(VI) ion and biomonitoring of cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: Phan, Le Minh Tu
Baek, Seung Hoon
Nguyen, Thang Phan
Park, Kyoung Yeol
Ha, Siyoung
Rafique, Rafia
Kailasa, Suresh Kumar
Park, Tae Jung
coredata.dc:description: A facile one-step synthetic approach was developed for fabrication of fluorescent silicon quantum dots (Si QDs) and used as a probe for fluorescence detection of hexavalent chromium (Cr (VI)) in environmental water samples. The as-prepared Si QDs exhibit a strong fluorescence emission peak at 520 nm with a quantum yield of 14.2%. The fluorescent Si QDs were rapidly produced by using ascorbic acid as a reductant at 55 °C. The emission peak of Si QDs at 420 nm was effectively quenched upon the addition of Cr(VI). The Si QDs acted as the best fluorescent probe for the detection of Cr(VI) at PBS pH 7.4. The developed probe possessed a good linear correlation (R2 = 0.992) between Cr(VI) concentration (1.25–40 μM) and the (F0-F)/F0 values with a detection limit of 0.65 μM. Furthermore, the Si QDs served as a bio-probe for fluorescence imaging of A549 lung cancer cells and cell viability results confirmed the good biocompatible nature of Si QDs. The as-fabricated Si QDs show several advantages such as rapidity, selectivity and biocompatibility for sensing of Cr(VI) and imaging of A549 cells, which opens a facile analytical platform for environmental and bioimaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Si QDs
Cr(VI) detection
Cell imaging
Fluorescence quenching
Water samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Si QDs as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Cr(VI)
Cell imaging using Si QD as bioimaging probes
Optimization of reaction temperature and time
Effect of Si QDs concentration, ionic strength and pH on the fluorescence spectra of Si QDs
Detection of Cr(VI) using Si QDs as a fluorescent probe
Sensing mechanism for detection of Cr(VI)
Analytical application of Si QDs for the detection of Cr(VI) in water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning
Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was carried out with the support of “Cooperative Research Program for Agriculture Science and Technology Development (Project No. PJ01280901)” Rural Development Administration, Republic of Korea (20160122) and Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT ( NRF-2017R1A2B4009581 ) and Future Planning (MSIP).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HR-TEM image (b) FT-IR spectrum of Si QDs.

(a) UV–visible absorption, emission spectrum of Si QDs when excited at 410 nm. Inset: Photographic image of Si QDs in day light and under UV-light irradiation at 365 nm. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of Si QDs at different excitation wavelengths. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) Emission spectra of Si QDs at different dilutions (pristine, 10- and 100-fold), (b) Effect of NaCl concentration (100–500 mM) and pH on the emission intensity of Si QDs.
Effect of reaction time on the fluorescence emission spectra of Si QDs with and without Cr(VI).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of Si QDs in the presence of Cr(VI) at various concentrations (1.25–40 μM) at PBS (pH 7.4). (b) The calibration graph was plotted between the concentration of Cr(VI) and (F0-F)/F0 for the detection of Cr(VI).
Selectivity of Si QDs towards Cr(VI), the concentrations of inorganic species were 40 μM. All experiments were performed in PBS (pH 7.4) and the excitation wavelength was 360 nm.

(a) Cytotoxicity studies of Si QDs at different concentrations (50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, μg/mL) on A549 lung cancer cells. (b) Differential interference contrast (DIC) and fluorescence images of A549 cell line after incubation with Si QDs and 4′,6‑diamidino‑2‑phenylindole (DAPI), respectively.
Schematic illustration for the detection of Cr(VI) and bioimaging using Si QDs as a fluorescent probe.
Comparison of the present method with the reported methods for the detection of Cr(VI).
Determination of Cr(VI) in DI water and tap water.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Standard deviations (SD) of measurements are calculated from three independent experiments.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of fluorescent silicon quantum dots for ultra-rapid and selective sensing of Cr(VI) ion and biomonitoring of cancer cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Institute of Interdisciplinary Convergence Research, Research Institute of Halal Industrialization Technology, Chung-Ang University, 84 Heukseok-ro, Dongjak-gu, Seoul 06974, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Institute of Halal Industrialization Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, S. V. National Institute of Technology, Surat 395 007, Gujarat, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: S. V. National Institute of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile one-step synthetic approach was developed for fabrication of fluorescent silicon quantum dots (Si QDs) and used as a probe for fluorescence detection of hexavalent chromium (Cr (VI)) in environmental water samples. The as-prepared Si QDs exhibit a strong fluorescence emission peak at 520 nm with a quantum yield of 14.2%. The fluorescent Si QDs were rapidly produced by using ascorbic acid as a reductant at 55 °C. The emission peak of Si QDs at 420 nm was effectively quenched upon the addition of Cr(VI). The Si QDs acted as the best fluorescent probe for the detection of Cr(VI) at PBS pH 7.4. The developed probe possessed a good linear correlation (R2 = 0.992) between Cr(VI) concentration (1.25–40 μM) and the (F0-F)/F0 values with a detection limit of 0.65 μM. Furthermore, the Si QDs served as a bio-probe for fluorescence imaging of A549 lung cancer cells and cell viability results confirmed the good biocompatible nature of Si QDs. The as-fabricated Si QDs show several advantages such as rapidity, selectivity and biocompatibility for sensing of Cr(VI) and imaging of A549 cells, which opens a facile analytical platform for environmental and bioimaging applications.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past several decades, the amount of heavy metals has been increased in the environment due to the rapid industrialization and other anthropogenic activities [1]. Chromium pollution is mainly due to the various industrial processes that includes coal-burning power plants, chrome plating, gold mining, volcanic emissions, and waste combustion [2]. Mostly, chromium exists in two oxidation states, i.e., Cr(III) and Cr(VI). Among these, Cr(VI) poses a serious threat to human health and environment compared to Cr(III), because of its high solubility and carcinogenic activity towards nucleic acids even at ultra-trace level [3]. Biochemical studies revealed that Cr(VI) ion was easily entered cell membranes and reduced to Cr(III), generating intermediate species of chromium, which leads to form the radicals of various chemical species [4]. Due to its high cellular uptake, it inhibits several biochemical pathways in cells, which can induce the toxicity to various cells and cause chemical-DNA damage via DNA-reactive mechanism [5]. As a result, the maximum allowable concentration for Cr(VI) in drinking water is at a lower micromolar level, which were set by many nations [6]. In this connection, the development of a simple and rapid analytical system is highly desirable for the detection of Cr(VI) in environmental and food samples.

Several analytical techniques including atomic absorption spectroscopy [7], UV–visible spectroscopy [8], fluorescence spectroscopy [9,10], high pressure liquid chromatography- inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [11], X-ray fluorescence spectrometry [12], and atomic emission spectrometry [13] have been used for the detection of Cr(IV) in various samples. These methods exhibited superior accuracy and precision. However, they usually suffer from disadvantages such as sophisticated pretreatment, requirement of professional operation, and also high-cost, which render them unsuitable for real-time field monitoring.

Recently, semiconductor nanocrystals, or quantum dots (QDs) have widely attracted in various fields of science because of their special optoelectronic properties including, strong absorption, high quantum yield, size-tunable photoluminescent (PL) emission, and high stability [14]. However, toxic inorganic precursors are commonly used in the synthesis of traditional QDs that may cause toxic effects to various biological systems [15]. Similarly, carbon dots have also been served as effective fluorescent probes for assaying of various metal ions including Cr(VI), Fe(III) and Al(III) ions in various samples [16–19]. Although the CDs-based fluorescence methods have proved as simple analytical platforms for assaying of various metal ions, unfortunately the sensing mechanisms required longer time to complete quenching/enhancing fluorescence signals, which paralyze their applications in environmental analysis. In this connection, silicon quantum dots (Si QDs) have attracted much attention in analytical sciences because of their advantages such as inert, nontoxic, abundant, and low-cost nanomaterials [20]. Furthermore, Si QDs exhibit efficient fluorescence emission with photoluminescence quantum yield up to 60–75% [21]. As a result, Si QDs have become highly promising materials in multi-disciplinary research including physics, chemistry and biology [22,23]. Several researchers have significantly focused on the development of Si QDs-based fluorescent sensors for the detection of various chemical species including glucose [24], Cu2+ ion [25,26], dopamine [27], and nitroaromatic compounds [28]. Moreover, Si QDs have emerged as alternative to group II–VI heavy metal-based QDs because of their non-toxic nature and biocompatibility, which make them as promising optical probes for various biomedical and bioimaging applications [29,30]. They have been used as potential candidates for various applications including, drug vehicles, and in vitro and in vivo imaging studies [31,32]. These methods were essentially required highly experimental risky chemical i.e., NaH and high temperature (~500 °C) to fabricate Si QDs [33,34]. Thus, it is necessary to use user friendly chemical as a reductant for fabrication of Si QDs for their applications in sensing and bioimaging.

Herein, we report one-step synthetic approach for fabrication of Si QDs by using [3‑(2‑aminoethylamino)propyl]trimethoxysilane (AEAPTMS) as a silicon source and l‑ascorbic acid (AA) as a reducing agent. The as-synthesized Si QDs are well dispersed in water and shown high photoluminescence quantum yield (14.2%). The Si QDs were used as a fluorescent sensor for ultra-rapid and selective detection of Cr(VI) in water samples via fluorescence quenching (“turn-off”) mechanism. The characteristic emission peak of Si QDs was selectively quenched by the addition of Cr(VI), confirming the strong interactions between Cr(VI) and functional groups on the surfaces of Si QDs (Scheme 1 ). Furthermore, the bioimaging application of Si QDs was also explored by evaluating their cytotoxicity and bioimaging studies on A549 lung cancer cells.

[3‑(2‑Aminoethylamino)propyl]trimethoxysilane was obtained from Alfa Aesar corporation (Ward Hill, MA). l‑ascorbic acid was procured from Samchun Chemical (Pyeongtaek, Korea). Potassium dichromate was purchased from Daejung Chemicals (Siheung, Korea). Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO). The metal ions solutions were prepared by using their nitrate, sulfate, or chloride salts. All reagents from commercial sources were of analytical grade and used without further purification. All solutions were prepared using deionized water (18.2 MΩ specific resistance) purified by a Milli-Q system (Millipore, Billerica, MA).

All the glasswares were thoroughly washed with deionized water, air-dried and then used for the experiments. Briefly, 2 mL of AEAPTMS was mixed with 8.0 mL deionized water under constant stirring at room temperature for 10 min. To this, 2.5 mL of 0.1 M ascorbic acid was added and then stirred for 30 min at 55 °C. The size and optical properties of Si QDs can be controlled by optimizing temperature and reaction time. The solution was cooled to room temperature naturally and then the resulting solution was dialyzed by using dialysis membrane (1000 Da MWCO, Spectrum Labs, Los Angeles, CA) for 6 h. The fabricated Si QDs were stored at 4 °C for further use. The as-synthesized Si QDs showed an intense blue color fluorescence under UV illumination at 365 nm. The synthesized Si QDs were characterized by spectroscopic (UV–visible, fluorescence and Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR)) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) techniques. To evaluate the effect of pH on emission peak intensity of Si QDs, we studied the emission spectra of Si QDs at different pHs. Furthermore, the effect of ionic strength on the emission peak intensity of Si QDs (1 mg/mL) was studied using NaCl concentration in the range of 100–500 mM.

The UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra of Si QDs were collected on a multi-mode microplate reader (96-well microplate, BioTek Synergy H1, Winooski, VT). HR-TEM image was measured by a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, 300 kV, JEM-3010, Jeol, Tokyo, Japan). FT-IR spectrum was obtained by using FT-IR spectrometer (Jasco 6600FV, Jasco, Tokyo, Japan). XPS spectrum was obtained by X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer system (K-alpha+, ThermoFisher Scientific, USA). The quantum yield of Si QDs was measured by using quinine sulfate as a reference standard (QY = 58% in 0.5 M H2SO4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Si QDs as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence sensing ability of Si QDs was examined by measuring the emission peak intensity of Si QDs upon the addition of various metal ions separately. Briefly, various metal ions (K(I), Na(I), Pb(II), Li(I), Mg(II), Cr(VI), Co(II), Ca(II), Mn(II), Zn(II), Cu(II), Ag(I), Cd(II), Fe(II), and Fe(III), 150 μL, 40 μM) solutions were added to Si QDs (0.5 mg/mL) separately at PBS solution (pH 7.4) and then vortexed for 5 s. The emission spectra of the above solutions were measured, and the analytical method was developed for the detection of Cr(VI) based on the spectral changes using fluorescent Si QDs as a probe.

The cell viability of Si QDs was performed by using CCK-8 kit (Dojindo Molecular Technologies, Japan) according to the manufacturer's instructions. Briefly, A549 lung cancer cells (3 × 105) were seeded in 96-well microplate reader and cultured overnight (37 °C for 24 h) in 5% CO2 humidified incubator. Then, the cells were treated with different concentrations of Si QDs (50, 100, 150, 500, 250, 300 μg/mL) for 24 h and then CCK-8 assay was performed. Since WST-8 was reduced to colored formazan by dehydrogenase activities in cells. As a result, the formazan showed maximum absorbance at 450 nm, which is directly related to the live cells. Therefore, the cell viability was estimated by measuring the absorbance of A549 cells-Si QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging using Si QD as bioimaging probes
ce:section.ce:para: For imaging studies, A549 lung cancer cells were cultured in 5% CO2 humidified incubator at 37 °C. The cultured A549 cells (1 × 105) were placed on cover slips and rinsed twice with PBS. The cells were treated with Si QDs for 1 h. The cover slips of Si QDs-treated A549 cells were mounted onto glass slides. The bio-imaging nature of Si QDs was examined by fluorescence microscopy (Automated cell imaging system, JuLI Stage, NanoEnTek, Seoul, Korea).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of reaction temperature and time
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of reaction temperature and time on the synthesis of fluorescent Si QDs is shown in Supporting Information of Fig. S1. The emission spectra of Si QDs were taken at different reaction times from 10 to 50 min at different temperatures from 45 °C to 55 °C. It can be noticed that the particle size of Si QDs was gradually decreased with increasing reaction time due to the formation of ultra-small Si QDs, reaching the minimum size at 30 min. After 30 min reaction time at 55 °C, the diameter of Si QDs was increased due to the changes in the molecular structure or assembly of Si QDs, which stimulates the growth of Si QDs with larger size. Therefore, we selected reaction temperature and time at 55 °C and 30 min as the best conditions for the synthesis of stable fluorescent Si QDs. The as-synthesized Si QDs exhibited strong fluorescence emission peak at 520 nm when excited at 410 nm, which reflected blue color fluorescence under UV light irradiation at 365 nm. These results confirm that the formation of fluorescent Si QDs using ascorbic acid as a reductant.

The morphology and shape of Si QDs were confirmed by HR-TEM. As shown in Fig. 1a, as-synthesized Si QDs are in spherical shape and well dispersed in water with an average size of 3.8 ± 1.3 nm. Furthermore, the hydrodynamic diameter of Si QDs was found to be 5.6 nm, which is higher than the HR-TEM data since DLS data includes the hydrated molecules on the surfaces of Si QDs (Fig. S2). To investigate the surface functional groups on the surfaces of Si QDs, the FT-IR spectrum of Si QDs was studied (Fig. 1b). The strong peak at 1590 cm−1 corresponds to the NH bending vibrations. The small peak at 1472 cm−1 belongs to O-Si-Dangling stretching. Moreover, a characteristic broad absorption band at ≈3100–3500 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and –NH2 groups. These results suggest that –NH2 and –OH groups are present on the surface of Si QDs. Due to the presence of -NH2 groups, Si QDs are well dispersed in water and produced stable colloids with a zeta potential of +41 mV. Electron dispersive X-ray spectra (EDX) spectrum of Si QDs reveals that the presence of Si, C, N, and O elements in Si QDs (Fig. S3). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was also studied for the confirmation of element states and surface chemistry of Si QDs (Fig. S4). The peaks at 286.7, 533.4, 401.3 and 103.6 eV were assigned to C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, and Si 2p, respectively (Fig. S4a). The high resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s shows peaks at 285.6, 286.4, 287.1, 287.7, and 289.0 eV, which belong to CSi, CC/CC, CN, COH/COC and CO groups of Si QDs, respectively (Fig. S4b). The peaks at 400.5, 401.4, 403.7, and 399.9 eV are attributed to the SiNSi, NC, SiNO and NH2 groups, respectively (Fig. S4c). The O 1s spectrum shows two peaks at 534.7 and 532.4 eV, which are assigned to the CO, SiO (Fig. S4d). Similarly, the peaks at 100, 102.2, 103.6 eV correspond to the SiC, SiN and SiO groups of Si QDs, respectively (Fig. S4e). These results suggest that the existence of Si QDs with different functional groups.

The absorption and emission properties of Si QDs were investigated as indicated by the typical UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra (Fig. 2 ). It can be noticed that the characteristic absorption peak was observed at 360 nm, which reflects that the synthesized Si QDs are well dispersed with uniform size and shape [35]. The emission peak of Si QDs was observed at 520 nm when excited at 410 nm (Fig. 2a). Similarly, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2a, the Si QDs exhibited light yellow color in daylight and emitted strong blue color under exposure to UV irradiation at 365 nm. In order to know the excitation dependent behavior of Si QDs, we measured the emission spectra of Si QDs at different excitation wavelengths from 310 nm to 470 nm (Fig. 2b). It can be noticed that the emission peak of Si QDs was shifted from 440 to 550 nm along with a concurrent increase and then intensity was decreased. The maximum emission intensity was observed at 520 nm when excited at 410 nm. Furthermore, the stability of Si QDs was confirmed by the measuring the fluorescence spectra of Si QD at different time intervals, indicating that the as-synthesized Si QDs are stable even after three-month storage at 4 °C. The quantum yield of Si QDs was calculated to be 14.2% by using quinine sulfate as the reference standard (Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Si QDs concentration, ionic strength and pH on the fluorescence spectra of Si QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Although Si QDs exhibit unique optoelectronic properties owing to the quantum confinement effect, the apparent properties of Si QDs are usually measured without any dilution. Before exploring the application of Si QDs as fluorescent probes, we have investigated fluorescence spectra of Si QDs without and with dilution (10- and 100-folds) (Fig. 3a). It was noticed that the emission spectrum of Si QDs is sensitive to the volume of water added into Si QDs. These results indicate that the dilution of Si QDs solution plays a key role on the fluorescence properties of Si QDs, and the intrinsic luminescence and the high polarity of nanoclusters may lead to the different emission phenomena [36–38]. As shown in Fig. 3a, the emission peak of Si QDs was blue-shifted from 445 nm to 420 nm by 10- and 100-fold dilution with water. This blue-shift may be attributed due to two reasons (i) energy-transfer process in QDs that related to selective fluorescence quenching and light scattering [39], and (ii) dilution of QDs solutions leads to either change or remove surface ligands on QDs, which may exhibit different emission phenomena [36]. Fig. S6 shows the emission peak intensities of Si QDs (10-, 50- and 100-fold) at different excitation wavelengths from 350 to 400 nm. It can be observed that the maximum emission peak intensity was observed at excitation wavelength of 360 nm, and then the intensity was decreased with blue-shift for three Si QDs (pristine, 10- and 100-fold). Based on these results, the analytical method was established by using Si QDs with 100-fold dilution.

It is well known that the ionic strength of solution is strongly influenced on the emission intensity of fluorescent nanomaterials. Fig. 3b shows the emission spectra of Si QDs using NaCl (100–500 mM) as an ionic strength. It can be noticed that the emission peak intensity at 420 nm was slightly decreased with increasing concentration of NaCl, suggesting that the quenching of fluorescence intensity via electrostatic interactions between the surfaces of Si QDs, which confirms the self-absorption minimization [36–38]. As shown in Fig. 3c, the emission intensity of Si QDs was low at acidic and basic pH (5.8, 6.4, 8.0, 9.0 and 10.0) because of protonation of -NH2 groups at acidic pH and the deprotonation of hydroxylic groups on the surface of Si QDs, resulting to develop “protective shell” with positive (acidic pH) and negative (basic pH) charges on the surface of Si QDs, which can cause to quench the emission of Si QDs. At the same time, the maximum emission intensity was observed at PBS pH 7.4, suggesting that Si QDs were stable and exhibited maximum intensity at PBS pH 7.4. Therefore, we selected PBS (pH 7.4) as an optimum pH for assaying of Cr(VI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr(VI) using Si QDs as a fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: Before establishing the calibration graph, the degree of fluorescence quenching of Si QDs was investigated with the addition of Cr(VI) at different time intervals from 0.5 to 35 min and the result was shown in Fig. 4 . It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity at 420 nm was quenched to 54% within 0.5 min, after that the quenching of fluorescence intensity is remained constant. Therefore, the spectral measurements were carried out at 0.5 min, suggesting that the probe exhibits the rapidity to readout Cr(VI) within 30 s.

Based on the FT-IR spectrum, it can be confirmed that the as-synthesized Si QDs consist -NH2 and –OH groups, which can be useful for sensing of meal ions. We further evaluated the recognition ability of Si QDs towards Cr(VI). As shown in Fig. 5a, the emission spectra of Si QDs were recorded upon the addition of various concentrations of Cr(VI) (1.25–40 μM). With the increase of Cr(VI) concentration, the fluorescence intensity of Si QDs at 420 nm is progressively decreased, and a good linear relationship (R
2 = 0.992) was displayed between the quenching efficiency (F0-F)/F0 and the concentration of Cr(VI) in the range of 1.25–40 μM (Fig. 5b), where F0 and F are emission intensities at 420 nm in the absence and presence of Cr(VI), respectively. The limit of detection was calculated based on the 3σ/slope, and found to be 0.65 μM. Table 1 shows the comparison of present method for the detection of Cr(VI) with various nanomaterials-based fluorescent probes. As can be seen that the detection limit compares favorably with the reported methods for the detection of Cr(VI). Furthermore, the LOD of the present method (0.65 μM) is lower than the permissible levels of Cr(VI) in water with 0.95 μM (0.05 mg/L) and 1.9 μM (0.1 mg/L), recommended by the World Health Organization and US Environmental Protection Agency, respectively [40,41] suggests that the present method meets the environmental agencies regulations for the detection of Cr(VI) in water samples. Importantly, this probe was effectively recognized Cr(VI) within 30 s, which shows that the rapidity of the method for the detection of Cr(VI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism for detection of Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the spectral observations, the as-synthesized Si QDs acted as a probe for fluorescence “turn-off” detection of Cr(VI). Since the surfaces of Si QD were fully covered with active organic functional groups (–COOH, –OH and –NH2), which can act as electron donor for effective chelation with Cr(VI). This results in quenching of fluorescence intensity due to the aggregation of Si QDs via the formation of Si QDs-Cr(VI) complexes (Scheme 1). Therefore, the emission intensity of Si QDs was quenched by the addition of small amounts of Cr(VI), which allows developing fluorescence “turn-off” method for assaying of Cr(VI). To confirm the fluorescence “turn-off” mechanism via Cr(VI) ion induced aggregation of Si QDs, various spectroscopic (DLS, XPS) techniques were used to study the spectral characteristics of Si QDs in the presence of Cr(VI). It can be noticed that the hydrodynamic diameter of Si QDs was greatly increased due to the aggregation of Si QDs induced by Cr(VI) (Fig. S7a). The characteristic binding energies of active functional groups were slightly changed due to the interaction of –NH2, –COOH and –OH groups with Cr(VI) (Fig. S8). These spectral data were in good agreement with the TEM image, which confirms the aggregation of Si QDs induced by Cr(VI) via the formation of Si QDs-Cr(VI) complexes (Fig. S7b). These results confirm that Cr(VI) can effectively bind with the surfaces of Si QDs via coordinate covalent bonds, which facilitates to develop “fluorescence turn-off” sensor for the detection of Cr(VI) (Scheme 1).

Selectivity is an important parameter to evaluate the anti-interference performance of the developed fluorescent probe. To confirm the selectively of the probe, the emission peak of Si QDs at 420 nm was studied upon the addition of various inorganic species (metal ions – K(I), Pb(II), Li(I), Mg(II), Co(II), Ca(II), Zn(II), Cu(II), Ag(I), Cd(II), Fe(II), Fe(III), Ba(II), Mn(II), Hg(II) and As(V); anions – F−, Cl−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, and NO3 −, and organic molecules – urea, citric acid, glucose, sucrose, methanol and ethanol, 40 μM) along with 40 μM of Cr(VI) (Fig. 6 ). The slight enhancement in the emission intensity of Si QDs was observed in the presence of K(I), Cd(II), and Pb(II) ions due to the adsorption of these metal ions onto the surfaces of Si QDs, which may lead to enhance fluorescence [19]. Overall, the above species did not influence on the detection of Cr(VI) using Si QDs as a fluorescent probe. Further, Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions were slightly interfered with the detection of Cr(VI), however, the both ions were effectively masked by using ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) as a masking agent. It was noticed that the quenching efficiency of Cr(VI) was slightly higher when EDTA added to the system. This is probably due to the formation of larger Si QDs-Cr(VI) nanostructured networks where EDTA can reduce the distance between Si QDs-Cr(VI) aggregates, which leads to more quenching than the Si QDs-Cr(VI). Moreover, the selectivity of Si QDs towards Cr(VI) was also investigated in the presence of higher concentrations of Cr(III) (Fig. S9). This result reveals that Cr(VI) (15 μM) was selectively quenched the emission peak of Si QDs even at higher concentrations of Cr(III), which signifies the selectivity of the probe for the detection of Cr(VI). Finally, the as-synthesized Si QDs acted as a selective probe for fluorescence “turn-off” detection of Cr(VI), which confirms that this probe has ability to detect Cr(VI) without any interference from the other chemical substances in the real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical application of Si QDs for the detection of Cr(VI) in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to know the practicality of the probe, the recovery experiments were performed in deionized and tap waters, and the results were shown in Table 2 . To this, the water samples (deionized and tap) were spiked with different concentration of Cr(VI) and then analyzed by the aforesaid procedure. These results indicate that the recoveries of Cr(VI) were in range of 84–113%, revealing the satisfactory consistencies between the actual concentrations and the measured results, which suggests that the present method may broaden ways for the detection of Cr(VI) environmental samples with minimized sample preparations.

The use of Si QDs as bioimaging probes was demonstrated by imaging of A549 lung cancer cell using fluorescence microscopy (Fig. 7 ). The cell viability was examined by CCK-8 assay. Fig. 7a shows the cell viability of A549 lung cancer cells after treating with different concentrations of Si QDs (50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300 μg/mL). It can be noticed that there was no reduction in the cell viability even at Si QDs concentration 300 μg/mL, revealing the biocompatible nature of Si QDs. Further, Si QDs were employed as fluorescent probes for bioimaging of A549 lung cancer cells. It can be noticed that the intracellular uptake of Si QDs by A549 lung cancer cells, resulting bright green fluorescence (Fig. 7b). This result indicates that Si QDs mainly through endocytosis mechanism. These imaging results revealed that the Si QDs have good dispersity and easily internalized by A549 lung cancer cells, yielding a bright green fluorescence, which confirms that the Si QDs served as fluorescent bio-probes for imaging of A549 lung cancer cells. These results indicate that the fluorescent bioimaging ability and non-toxic nature of Si QDs allow them as promising bioprobes for cell imaging and biomedical applications.

In summary, we employed a simple one-step synthetic approach for the synthesis of Si QDs using AEAPTMS as a silicon source and AA as a reducing agent. The ultra-small fluorescent Si QDs were rapidly procedure without applying high temperatures, and this method avoids the use of dangerous reductant i.e., NaH. The synthesized Si QDs are well dispersed in water with an average size of 3.8 ± 1.3 nm and exhibited blue emission under UV light irradiation. The as-prepared Si QDs acted as a fluorescent probe for the selective detection of Cr(VI) with minimal volume of samples. Moreover, the Si QDs-based fluorescent sensor eliminates the use of semiconductor quantum dots, other organic dyes, revealing that the present probe meets green chemistry approach. Most importantly, Si QDs shows the promising practical application for the detection of Cr(VI) in water samples, and the LOD of the methods meets the environmental protection agencies permissible levels for Cr(VI) in water samples. The fluorescence “turn-off” mechanism was rapidly observed with the addition of Cr(VI), which reveals that this probe may offer a novel platform for the ultra-rapid detection of Cr(VI) in environmental samples at low volume samples without any tedious sample preparations. Furthermore, the application of Si QDs as non-toxic fluorescent probes has been examined on A549 lung cancer cells, revealing that the Si QDs have good dispersity and biocompatibility towards cells. The use of user friendly reagents in synthesizing Si QDs and rapid fluorescence “turn-off” mechanism make Si QDs as potential fluorescent probes for sensing of Cr(VI) and imaging of A549 cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was carried out with the support of “Cooperative Research Program for Agriculture Science and Technology Development (Project No. PJ01280901)” Rural Development Administration, Republic of Korea (20160122) and Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT (NRF-2017R1A2B4009581) and Future Planning (MSIP).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2018.08.024.
